narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72.9% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, and background details like your upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, without considering gender or race. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group—far below the middle-upper deciles typical of students who passed the bar—strongly signaling a higher risk of failure. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level compared to the much higher averages for successful bar takers, further pushing the prediction toward expecting struggles ahead. Your LSAT score offered some counterbalance, as it was reasonably solid and pulled the odds slightly in favor of passing, though not enough to offset the other concerns. Overall, these early academic deciles stood out as the dominant factors, unlike the stronger Year 1 and Year 3 performances seen in most admitted students who went on to pass the bar. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the 3.24 average for passers, and your upper-middle income aligns well with about 45% of them, but they weren't decisive here. While your full-time status matches the vast majority of successful students, it didn't sway the outcome significantly. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven forecast based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score or strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program, then reapply with that refreshed profile. Many students in similar positions have improved their trajectories and succeeded on later attempts. You're capable of turning this around with targeted steps.
